---
title: "The Model That Barely Uses Its Own Brain"
date: 2025-07-03
sidebar: true
---


```{=html}
<script>document.body.classList.add('book-page');</script>
```

In the autumn of 1991, long before the word "transformer" entered the vocabulary of machine learning,
long before GPUs became programmable and decades before anyone imagined training models with
hundreds of billions of parameters, Robert Jacobs and his colleagues proposed an idea that was, at the
time, both elegant and strangely impractical. Instead of constructing a single monolithic neural network
responsible for solving every problem, why not build a collection of specialized networks and allow
another network to decide which of those specialists should handle a particular input?


The proposal was called a Mixture of Experts .


Today the idea seems almost obvious. Hospitals do not assign every physician to every patient.
Universities do not ask every professor to teach every course. Modern societies derive much of their
efficiency not from universal competence but from specialization. Expertise is expensive to acquire, and
once acquired, it becomes economically irrational to deploy all experts for every task. A dermatologist
need not attend a routine eye examination; an astrophysicist need not review a tax return. Institutions
become scalable precisely because they learn to activate only the expertise that is relevant.


Yet for much of the history of deep learning, neural networks operated according to precisely the
opposite principle.


Every neuron participated in every forward pass.


Every parameter contributed to every prediction.


Every token activated the entire network.


This architectural choice remained largely unquestioned, not because it was necessarily optimal, but
because for many years it simply wasn't the limiting factor. Dense computation was straightforward to
implement, GPUs were designed to excel at regular matrix operations, and models remained
sufficiently small that the inefficiencies were tolerable. The brute-force approach worked well enough
that few researchers had compelling reasons to revisit an alternative proposed decades earlier.


The remarkable success of the Transformer only reinforced this trajectory. BERT, GPT, PaLM, Llama and
their contemporaries all demonstrated that larger dense networks consistently produced better
capabilities. Every successive generation increased parameter counts by one or two orders of
magnitude, and for a time the economics appeared entirely favorable. Storage became cheaper.
Accelerators became faster. Scaling laws rewarded additional computation. If activating every
parameter produced better intelligence, why complicate the architecture?


The answer emerged not from mathematics but from economics.


Imagine a library containing one million books. Suppose a researcher visits in search of a single paper
on distributed consensus algorithms. It would be absurd to insist that the librarian retrieve every
volume in the building before allowing the researcher to read the one that mattered. The inefficiency is
obvious because physical movement dominates the task. The objective is not to maximize the number
of books handled but to minimize unnecessary work.


Dense transformers perform a surprisingly similar operation.


Consider a model with six hundred billion parameters. Every generated token activates every feed-
forward layer, every projection matrix and every learned representation, regardless of whether the
model is correcting punctuation, translating French poetry, generating SQL queries or explaining the
Byzantine Generals Problem. The overwhelming majority of the model's representational capacity
remains relevant only in a statistical sense; much of it is unlikely to contribute meaningfully to the
immediate prediction. Nevertheless, the architecture insists upon mobilizing the entire network for
every token.


This observation becomes increasingly uncomfortable as models grow larger.


Suppose one doubles the parameter count of a dense model. Storage requirements double. Training
cost approximately doubles. Inference cost also grows because every parameter participates in every
forward pass. Capacity and computation remain tightly coupled. Intelligence becomes more expressive,
certainly, but it also becomes proportionally more expensive to use.


The relationship is deceptively simple:


More parameters require more computation.


For years this coupling appeared almost inevitable.


Mixture of Experts proposed severing it.


The central insight behind sparse expert architectures is not that larger models are inherently better,
nor that specialization itself is novel. Rather, it is that capacity and computation need not scale together . A
model may contain hundreds of billions—or even trillions—of parameters while activating only a small
subset for any individual token. Capacity continues to grow, but computation increases much more
slowly.


The distinction is subtle enough that it is often lost in popular discussions. People frequently describe
MoE models as "only using a few experts," as though sparsity were the innovation. It is not. Sparsity is
merely the mechanism. The deeper idea is economic: expensive resources should only be consumed
when they create value.


A useful comparison can be drawn with cloud computing. Modern datacenters rarely dedicate an entire
physical server to every application. Instead, workloads are scheduled dynamically across shared
infrastructure, allocating resources only when required. The efficiency arises not because the servers
themselves are more powerful but because idle capacity has become economically unacceptable.
Sparse neural networks embody the same philosophy. Parameters may exist without being
continuously active.


Once viewed through this lens, Mixture of Experts ceases to look like a clever neural network
architecture and begins to resemble a resource scheduling problem.


That realization, however, immediately introduces another difficulty.


Who decides which expert should process a token?


The routing network, sometimes described as the gate, performs this assignment by estimating which
experts are likely to contribute most effectively to the current input. Conceptually, the mechanism is
straightforward. Operationally, it is one of the most challenging components in the entire architecture.


An ideal router should satisfy several objectives simultaneously. It should consistently dispatch
semantically similar inputs toward experts capable of handling them effectively. It should avoid
overloading a small subset of experts while leaving others idle. It should minimize communication
across devices in distributed environments. Above all, it must accomplish these goals without
introducing computational overhead that outweighs the savings obtained through sparsity.


This last requirement proved unexpectedly difficult.


Early Mixture-of-Experts systems frequently suffered from what researchers now refer to as expert
collapse . The router discovered that a small number of experts consistently produced lower training loss
and consequently directed an ever-increasing proportion of tokens toward them. Popular experts
became overloaded while others received insufficient data to learn meaningful representations. The
architecture gradually converged toward the very dense computation it had been designed to avoid.


For nearly three decades, these problems prevented Mixture of Experts from becoming more than an
intellectually attractive concept. The idea was correct. The surrounding ecosystem was not.


Several independent developments had to occur before sparse architectures became practical.


Accelerators needed sufficient memory capacity to store enormous collections of experts. High-speed
interconnects such as NVLink and InfiniBand reduced, though did not eliminate, the communication
penalties associated with routing tokens across devices. Distributed training frameworks became
sophisticated enough to schedule dynamic execution efficiently. Routing algorithms improved. Auxiliary
load-balancing losses became better understood. Compiler technologies matured. Even the economics
of frontier model development changed. Once training runs began consuming millions of GPU-hours,
recovering even modest improvements in utilization translated directly into millions of dollars in
savings.


Only then did the original insight reveal its full significance.


The success of models such as DeepSeek V3, Qwen's larger sparse variants, and Kimi K2 should
therefore not be interpreted merely as evidence that Mixture of Experts "works." The more interesting
observation is that these laboratories treated sparse computation not as an architectural novelty but as
the organizing principle around which the remainder of the system could be designed. Expert routing,
communication overlap, memory layout, distributed execution and optimizer behavior ceased to be
independent engineering problems. They became different expressions of the same optimization
objective: activate precisely the computation that is necessary and avoid everything else.


DeepSeek's contribution illustrates this philosophy particularly well. Much attention has focused on the
sheer scale of the model—hundreds of billions of total parameters with only a fraction activated for
each token—but the parameter count itself is almost a distraction. The harder engineering challenge
lay in making sparse execution sufficiently efficient that its theoretical advantages survived contact with
real hardware. Token routing had to remain balanced. Communication overhead had to be hidden
beneath useful computation. Experts had to specialize without fragmenting. Idle GPUs had to become increasingly rare. Sparse computation, it turned out, demanded extraordinarily dense systems
engineering.


This inversion is worth pausing to appreciate.


The world's largest language models became possible not because engineers found a way to perform
more computation, but because they became increasingly skilled at deciding **which computation**
**could safely be omitted** .


That distinction may prove to be one of the defining ideas of modern artificial intelligence.


Yet even Mixture of Experts leaves another question unanswered.


Suppose one activates only a handful of experts for each token, dramatically reducing unnecessary
computation. The model still faces another, equally stubborn constraint. Every generated token must
repeatedly retrieve an ever-growing history from memory before those experts can perform useful
work. Sparse computation reduces how much the model thinks.


It does not reduce how much the model must remember.


That problem would require a very different kind of innovation—one that began not by changing the
network itself, but by questioning whether the information flowing through it needed to exist in its
original form at all.
